sources.title: Reconstructing 4Q208–4Q209 as an Astronomical Artefact
sources.author: Helen R. Jacobus
sources.summary: This thesis reconstructs the calendrical system of 4Q208-4Q209 (Aramaic Astronomical Book of Enoch) as a luni-solar calendar with zodiacal gates, relating it to Babylonian astronomical traditions and arguing it represents a synchronistic calendar different from the 364-day year.
sources.content: 4Q208–4Q209 comprise formulaically written texts that are part of the so called Aramaic Astronomical Book of Enoch from Qumran. I have also argued that 4Q208–4Q209 (4QAstronomical Enocha-b) follows a similar pattern to 4Q318: that it is basically a luni-solar calendar with the major modification that the cosmological 'gates' 1 to 6 should be identified as the zodiac signs. Neugebauer defined the heavenly gates in 1 En. 72 as the sun's rising and setting points on the horizon during the year: the sun rises in Gate 4 at the spring and autumn equinoxes (Month 1 and Month 6). He contended that the synchronistic calendar of 4Q208–4Q209 was the equivalent mathematically to 364 days of three ('solar') years equalling three lunar years of 354 days.

evidences.claim: The Astronomical Book describes a 364-day synchronistic calendar with heavenly gates corresponding to zodiac signs
evidences.corroboration: This source provides detailed reconstruction of 4Q208-4Q209 as a luni-solar calendar with zodiacal gates, confirming Neugebauer's interpretation of the gates as solar rising/setting points and the synchronistic calendar system
evidences.notes: PhD thesis providing comprehensive analysis of the calendrical system

sources.title: The Books of Enoch: Aramaic Fragments of Qumrân Cave 4
sources.author: J.T. Milik
sources.summary: The foundational critical edition of the Aramaic Enoch fragments from Qumran Cave 4, including 4Q201-4Q202 (Book of Watchers), 4Q208-4Q211 (Astronomical Book), and other manuscripts, with paleographical analysis dating them to late 3rd-2nd century BCE.
sources.content: THE BOOKS OF ENOCH ARAMAIC FRAGMENTS OF QUMRAN CAVE 4 EDITED BY J.T. MILIK WITH THE COLLABORATION OF MATTHEW BLACK. The year 1971 was the 150th anniversary of the first complete translation of the Ethiopic Enoch into a European language. The Enoch Scroll of the texts from Qumran Library Cave 4 has provided parts in Aramaic among the Dead Sea Scrolls discovery between 1947 and 1956. Contents: Aramaic Book of Enoch, Astronomical Book, Book of Watchers, Book of Dreams, Book of Giants, Enochic Writings.

evidences.claim: The Book of Watchers is attested in Aramaic fragments 4Q201-4Q202 dating to late 3rd-2nd century BCE
evidences.corroboration: This is Milik's definitive critical edition of the Aramaic fragments, providing paleographical analysis and dating of 4Q201-4Q202 as copies of the Book of Watchers from the late 3rd-2nd century BCE
evidences.notes: Foundational scholarly edition for all Aramaic Enoch studies

sources.title: The Books of Enoch: Aramaic Fragments of Qumrân Cave 4
sources.author: J.T. Milik
sources.summary: Internet Archive hosting of Milik's 1976 Oxford edition, the standard critical edition of Aramaic Enoch fragments from Qumran including paleographical plates and critical apparatus.
sources.content: The Books of Enoch : Aramaic fragments of Qumrân Cave 4 by Milik, J. T. (Józef Tadeusz), 1922-2006. Publication date 1976. Topics Ethiopic book of Enoch -- Criticism, Textual, Dead Sea scrolls, Books of Enoch -- Criticism, Textual. Commentary in English; text in English and Aramaic. Includes bibliographical references and indexes. Texts, translations, and notes: The books of Enoch. -- The astronomical book of Enoch. -- The book of giants. -- Appendix: Diplomatic transcription of 4QEn a-g.

evidences.claim: Milik's edition establishes the paleographical dating and textual reconstruction of the Aramaic Watchers fragments
evidences.corroboration: This source provides the complete critical apparatus for 4Q201-4Q202 and other Aramaic fragments, establishing their paleographical dating to the late 3rd-2nd century BCE and their relationship to the Ethiopic text
evidences.notes: Standard reference work for Aramaic Enoch studies

sources.title: Scrolls fragments - Qumran manuscripts index
sources.summary: University of Pennsylvania index of Qumran scroll fragments including detailed descriptions of 4Q201-4Q207 (Enoch manuscripts) with references to Milik's edition and content descriptions.
sources.content: 4Q201 (4QEna ar) 4QEnoch a - Milik, Enoch. Aramaic Fragments from Qumran Cave 4 (Oxford 1976) 139-163, 340-343, pls I-V. Copy of the Book of Watchers from 1 Enoch. Remains of 1 En 1:1-6; 2:1-5.6; 6:4-8:1; 8:3-9:3; 9:6-8; 10:3-4.21-11:1; 12:4-6; 14:4-6. 4Q202 (4QEnb ar) 4QEnoch b - Milik, Enoch, 164-178, 344-346, pls. VI-IX. Another copy of the Book of Watchers from 1 Enoch. Remains of En 5:9-6:4; 6:7-8:1; 8:2-9:4; 10:8-12.

evidences.claim: 4Q201 and 4Q202 are Aramaic copies of the Book of Watchers containing specific chapter ranges
evidences.corroboration: This source provides detailed content descriptions of 4Q201-4Q202, confirming they are copies of the Book of Watchers with specific verse ranges from 1 Enoch 1-14
evidences.notes: University academic resource with manuscript descriptions

sources.title: The Animal Apocalypse (1 Enoch 83-90), with Aramaic Fragments and translations
sources.summary: Open Siddur Project presentation of the Animal Apocalypse with Ge'ez text and English translation, dating it to the Maccabean struggle (165-161 BCE) and explaining its symbolic animal imagery.
sources.content: The following work, chapters 83-90 in Sefer Ḥanokh (Book of Enoch), dates from the period of the Maccabean struggle, circa 165-161 BCE. It is a remarkable chronicle presenting world history as a predatory conflict. All created beings, from angels to humans, are demoted, one step as it were, to human beings and animals. The people of Israel are presented as domesticated creatures — sheep — and her enemies as predators. The story emphasizes the theme found earlier in Ḥanokh and elsewhere in Jewish tradition which identifies the yetser hara as the inclination towards predation.

evidences.claim: The Animal Apocalypse dates to the Maccabean period (165-161 BCE) and uses symbolic animal imagery to represent historical conflict
evidences.corroboration: This source provides the traditional dating of the Animal Apocalypse to the Maccabean struggle and explains the symbolic system where Israelites are sheep and enemies are predators
evidences.notes: Open access translation project with scholarly introduction

sources.title: A commentary on the Animal apocalypse of I Enoch
sources.author: Patrick A. Tiller
sources.summary: Comprehensive scholarly commentary on the Animal Apocalypse (1 Enoch 85-90) providing detailed analysis of its symbolism, chronology, and historical context within Second Temple Judaism.
sources.content: A commentary on the Animal apocalypse of I Enoch by Patrick A. Tiller. Publication date 1993. Topics Ethiopic book of Enoch LXXXV-XC -- Criticism, interpretation, etc, Bible. Old Testament -- History of Biblical events, Animals -- Symbolic aspects, Animals -- Religious aspects -- Judaism, Jews -- History -- To 70 A.D. Revision of the author's dissertation (Ph.D.)--Harvard Divinity School, 1991. Includes text of the Animal apocalypse with translation and bibliographical references.

evidences.claim: The Animal Apocalypse is a symbolic history using animal imagery to represent Israel's history from creation to eschaton
evidences.corroboration: This scholarly commentary provides detailed analysis of the Animal Apocalypse's symbolic system, its animal imagery representing different historical figures and nations, and its eschatological framework
evidences.notes: Specialized academic commentary from Harvard dissertation

sources.title: Introduction to The Animal Apocalypse: 1 Enoch 85-90
sources.author: Jason Lovelace
sources.summary: Academic overview of the Animal Apocalypse explaining its allegorical representation of history through animal imagery, its Maccabean dating (165-160 BCE), and its theological themes.
sources.content: This essay provides an overview of the 'Animal Vision' or 'Animal Apocalypse,' one of the most striking sections of 1 Enoch. Scholars date its composition to 165-160 BC during the Maccabean revolt, based on Qumran fragments. The narrative features a chronological retelling of biblical events, with selective omissions illustrating a deliberate strategy. Key theological themes include criticism of the Second Temple and the eschatological role of the Maccabean revolt. The allegory emphasizes a transformative vision, uniting Jews and Gentiles in eschatological blessings.

evidences.claim: The Animal Apocalypse was composed during the Maccabean revolt (165-160 BCE) and contains historical allegory critical of the Second Temple
evidences.corroboration: This source confirms the Maccabean dating based on Qumran fragments and explains the text's criticism of the Second Temple and its eschatological vision centered on the Maccabean revolt
evidences.notes: Academic overview on open access platform

sources.title: The Nature of the Beast: The Animal Apocalypse(s) of Enoch
sources.author: Martha Himmelfarb
sources.summary: Princeton dissertation challenging the consensus that the entire Animal Apocalypse belongs to the Maccabean period, proposing that the Seventy-Shepherd Schema belongs to first-century CE Roman Judaea and the First Jewish Revolt.
sources.content: This project reimagines the textual history of the Animal Apocalypse, a subsection of an early Jewish work called 1 Enoch, which narrates a history of the Israelites and their adversaries stretching from Eden to the eschaton in which all are represented as different species of animals. The dissertation disrupts the scholarly consensus that the entirety of the Animal Apocalypse belongs to the Maccabean revolt, and proposes that a crucial piece of it belongs in first-century CE Roman Judaea—specifically, to the world of the First Jewish Revolt.

evidences.claim: The Animal Apocalypse may have multiple layers of composition, with some sections possibly dating to the First Jewish Revolt (66-70 CE) rather than exclusively Maccabean period
evidences.corroboration: This source challenges the traditional Maccabean dating, proposing that the Seventy-Shepherd Schema may belong to the First Jewish Revolt period, offering a more complex textual history
evidences.notes: Recent dissertation proposing new dating framework

sources.title: Selections from 1 Enoch - Apocalypse of Weeks
sources.summary: St. Mary's University academic resource providing the text of the Apocalypse of Weeks (1 Enoch 93:1-10; 91:11-17) with the 'plant of righteousness' motif and periodization of history into ten weeks.
sources.content: The Apocalypse of Weeks (93:1-10; 91:11-17). And Enoch took up his discourse and said, 'I was born the seventh in the first week, and until my time righteousness endured. After me there will arise a second week, in which deceit and violence will spring up, and in it will be the first end, and in it a man will be saved. After this there will a third week, and at its conclusion a man will be chosen as the plant of righteous judgment, and after him will go forth the plant of righteousness forever and ever.'

evidences.claim: The Apocalypse of Weeks divides history into ten periods with the 'plant of righteousness' appearing in the third week
evidences.corroboration: This source provides the actual text of the Apocalypse of Weeks showing the periodization scheme and the 'plant of righteousness' motif in week three, confirming the eschatological framework
evidences.notes: University academic resource with primary text

sources.title: The Apocalypse of Weeks: Periodization and Tradition-Historical Context
sources.summary: Scholarly analysis of the Apocalypse of Weeks examining its periodization of history into ten weeks, the 'plant of righteousness' motif, and its tradition-historical context within Second Temple Jewish apocalypticism.
sources.content: The time covered in aw is comprehensive: it embraces events spanning from the primordial era before the great flood all the way up to and even beyond the eschatological future. The measured part of history is divided into ten 'weeks.' The term, which draws attention to the principled significance of the number seven, denotes predetermined periods of time. Within this scheme, aw is highly selective in choosing from a number of larger pools of possibilities. Its brief descriptions of events to occur in each week mark out one or more occurrences that are deemed to be either bad or good, and, thus, are implicitly exhortational. The events, which reflect matters of particular concern to the writer, reflect something of what the writer regards as the ideal community, as indicated by the nomenclature 'plant of righteousness' in weeks three and seven.

evidences.claim: The 'plant of righteousness' motif appears in weeks three and seven, representing the ideal community and Abraham's election
evidences.corroboration: This source analyzes the periodization structure of the Apocalypse of Weeks and explains the significance of the 'plant of righteousness' motif as representing the ideal community and Abraham's election in the third week
evidences.notes: Academic analysis of periodization and motifs

sources.title: The Book of Enoch (Charles)/Chapter 93 - Apocalypse of Weeks
sources.author: R.H. Charles
sources.summary: Wikisource text of R.H. Charles' translation of 1 Enoch chapter 93 containing the Apocalypse of Weeks with the 'plant of righteousness' motif and the ten-week periodization scheme.
sources.content: XCIII, XCI. 12-17. The Apocalypse of Weeks. And after that Enoch both gave and began to recount from the books. And Enoch said: 'Concerning the children of righteousness and concerning the elect of the world, And concerning the plant of uprightness, I will speak these things, Yea, I Enoch will declare (them) unto you, my sons.' And after that in the third week at its close a man shall be elected as the plant of righteous judgment, And his posterity shall become the plant of righteousness for evermore.

evidences.claim: The Apocalypse of Weeks introduces the 'plant of uprightness' and 'plant of righteousness' as key motifs in the periodization scheme
evidences.corroboration: This source provides Charles' translation showing the 'plant of uprightness' and 'plant of righteousness' motifs in their textual context within the ten-week periodization framework
evidences.notes: Public domain translation of scholarly standard

sources.title: The Book of Enoch: Chapter 93 - Apocalypse of Weeks
sources.summary: Internet Sacred Text Archive presentation of 1 Enoch chapter 93 with the Apocalypse of Weeks, including the 'plant of righteousness' motif and the ten-week historical division.
sources.content: XCIII, XCI. 12-17. The Apocalypse of Weeks. And after that Enoch both gave and began to recount from the books. And Enoch said: 'Concerning the children of righteousness and concerning the elect of the world, And concerning the plant of uprightness, I will speak these things, Yea, I Enoch will declare (them) unto you, my sons.' And after that in the third week at its close a man shall be elected as the plant of righteous judgment, And his posterity shall become the plant of righteousness for evermore.

evidences.claim: The third week features the election of a figure as the 'plant of righteous judgment' whose posterity becomes the eternal 'plant of righteousness'
evidences.corroboration: This source confirms the textual details of the 'plant of righteousness' motif in week three and its connection to the election of a righteous figure and his posterity
evidences.notes: Online text archive with standard translation
